title: Euclidean Distance
concept: Straight-line distance between vectors and when it behaves differently than angle-based metrics.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about the dot product. What happens to the dot product when we normalize our embedding vectors to a length of 1?

options.text: The dot product measures the sum of the magnitudes.

exercises.explain: When vectors are normalized (scaled to a length of 1.0), the dot product depends entirely on the angle between them, making it mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

cards.title: The Straight-Line Metric
cards.body: While cosine similarity cares only about the angle between vectors, sometimes the actual size and strength of those vectors matter. Euclidean distance helps us measure the true physical gap between points.

cards.title: What is Euclidean Distance?
cards.body: Euclidean distance is the straight-line distance between two points in a multi-dimensional space. Unlike cosine similarity which looks at direction, Euclidean distance measures physical separation.

cards.title: The Rope Analogy
cards.body: Imagine two hikers climbing a mountain. Cosine similarity only checks if they are climbing in the same direction. Euclidean distance measures the actual physical length of a rope tied straight between them.

cards.title: Scale vs. Direction
cards.body: In a database of houses, a tiny 1-bedroom starter home and a massive 5-bedroom mansion might both point in the 'suburban family home' direction, but Euclidean distance highlights how far apart they are in scale.

cards.title: Closer Isn't Always Better
cards.body: You might think a smaller Euclidean distance always means higher semantic similarity. However, if your texts are of wildly different lengths, their vectors will sit far apart in space even if their topics are identical!

cards.title: Angle vs. Distance
cards.body: See how different metrics focus on different qualities of vectors in space.

left.text: Measures only the angle. Ignores how long the vectors are.

right.text: Measures the straight-line gap. Strongly affected by vector length.

exercises.prompt: How does Euclidean distance behave differently than cosine similarity?

options.text: It only measures the angle between vectors.
It is highly sensitive to the magnitude (length) of the vectors.
It ignores the coordinate values of the points.
It always equals the dot product.

exercises.explain: Euclidean distance is sensitive to vector magnitude (length), whereas cosine similarity only measures the angle between vectors regardless of their length.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If all your embedding vectors are normalized to have a magnitude of 1, ranking them by Euclidean distance yields the same order as ranking them by cosine similarity.
exercises.explain: If two vectors are normalized to a length of 1, their endpoints sit on a unit sphere. In this specific case, a smaller straight-line distance always corresponds to a smaller angle.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how Euclidean distance measures the path between two embeddings:
exercises.sentence: Euclidean distance measures the straight-line distance between two vector points.
exercises.explain: Euclidean distance calculates the shortest straight-line path between two points in vector space.

exercises.prompt: Identify the statement that sounds like it was generated by an AI assistant:

options.text: I prefer using Euclidean distance when the actual size of the data values matters, like comparing house prices and sizes.
To determine the similarity, compute the Euclidean distance between Point A and Point B using the Pythagorean formula.
If you normalize your vectors first, minimizing Euclidean distance gives you the same ranking as maximizing cosine similarity.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an AI assistant because it uses overly structured, robotic phrasing containing placeholder-style text like 'Point A' and 'Point B' instead of natural language.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you have two documents that discuss the exact same topic using the same proportions of words, but Document A is a 10-word summary and Document B is a 1000-word essay. Why will they have a large Euclidean distance?

options.text: The difference in document length scales up the vector magnitudes, creating a large physical distance between them.
Angle-based metrics will also show them as highly distant.
Euclidean distance is only affected by the number of unique vocabulary words, not document length.

exercises.explain: Euclidean distance measures absolute distance. If two documents have identical word patterns but one is a short tweet and the other is a long blog post, their embedding vectors will have very different magnitudes. This creates a large Euclidean distance between them even though their topic (angle) is identical.
